questions.description: "Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of ultra… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: "Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of ultra vires has been abolished for LTDs, to which Parts 2 to 15 apply, but remains in place for DACs, PLCs, CLGs and UCs". You are required to discuss and explain this statement. Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions. Jackie's business has failed. For the last 6 years, Jackie operated a flower shop from a unit in a shopping center outside of Dublin. The business was conducted through her company, which is now called Smell that DAC (the "Company") of which Jackie was the sole shareholder, and one of two directors, the other director being Jackie's former fiancé, Ted. Jackie contacted Ted to tell him the company was insolvent, and they agreed to hold a directors' meeting with a view to putting the company into creditors' voluntary winding up. On meeting with Jackie and Ted, you elicit the following information: - The company now owes €500,000 in back rent and taxes. The company has struggled through the recession, as people have had less disposable income to spend on Flowers. The final straw has been an increase in rent on the shop unit. The company rents, which was increased from €20,000 to €40,000 per month. The - Revenue Commissioners have not been paid VAT on sales or PRSI or PAYE due on Jackie's salary from the Company for three months. - In addition, the revenue Commissioners have issued an assessment for €50,000 in relation to back tax owed to them, which the company cannot afford to pay. The circumstances in which this has become due were that Jackie had hired a teenage girl to work a few hours on a Saturday morning, paying her cash in hand. When this was discovered by the Revenue Commissioners, they claimed for lost PRSI and PAYE tax on the girl's earnings over a two-year period. - The Company's books and records have been maintained by a local bookkeeper to an acceptable standard, however, on the way to delivering the books and records to the bookkeeper for last 12-month period, Jackie's car was stolen, and the books and records destroyed when joy-rider thieves of set fire to the car. - Ted, the Company's second director, has not been active in the business of the Company. He agreed to be a director whilst engaged to Jackie because every company needs two directors, but he has not been active in the company's business apart from signing accounts and forms when asked to do so. Although they since split off, the breakup was amicable but Ted had only agreed to stay on until Jackie could find someone else to become a director. - The liquidator has been looking to meet with them both but to date, while Jackie has met with him, Ted has not, having left a message with the liquidator's office to say "there is nothing I can help you with". You are required to advise both Jackie and Ted on the likelihood that they will be restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, and in particular on the defences available to them. Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars4all Ltd, (the "Company"), which operates a new-… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars4all Ltd, (the "Company"), which operates a new-car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company, which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by Simon, although Paul has worked in the company for 10 years; first as a car salesman, before being promoted on a number of occasions, until finally being appointed a director of the company with responsibility for sales. There are three other directors (all family members, making four directors in total). Simon, the majority shareholder and managing Director of the company, approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been operating privately a business on the side for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Pauline. Specifically, Simon heard that: Pauline has been buying second-hand cars from the Company (these are cars which have been traded in by customers of the Company against the purchase price of a new car); Paul has facilitated the sales of those second-hand cars at a discounted price before selling them on for a significant profit on their website; and Paul has been using the Company's garage workshop to service the cars late at night and on Sundays when the garage-workshop is closed. He has also used spare parts belonging to the Company but has not reimbursed the Company for these. You are required to advise Simon on the following: (a) Whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Paul, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; [and]{.underline} (b) The remedies that are available to the company arising from any breach of duties. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: "The causes of crystallization of a floating charge are now well settled in Irish case law".
questions.text: "The causes of crystallization of a floating charge are now well settled in Irish case law". You are required to discuss this statement. Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

"Generally speaking, this principle [I.e. that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members] and the statutory rules of company law in which the principle is implicit apply to the relationship between holding companies and subsidiaries and to transactions between them and third parties" - Re Frederick Inns LTD [1991] ILRM 582 (HC) at 587 (Lardner J). You are required to discuss this application of the principle of separate corporate personality in the [specific context of holding companies and subsidiaries]{.underline}, Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line wine shop operating from… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line wine shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the "Bank") secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the bank can freeze at its discretion. Both charges were created in 2016. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which then appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Following her appointment, the Company's liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: (a) Through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favor of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under Section 409 of the Companies Act 2014; and (b) The charge over the book debts [was]{.underline} registered under Section 409; however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was [not]{.underline} registered under Section 409 and the liquidator told you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of sum due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) [which must be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks.]{.underline} Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.description: "A statutory predecessor of Section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 has been described as giving rise to a statutory…
questions.text: "A statutory predecessor of Section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 has been described as giving rise to a statutory contract of the most sacred character". You are required to discuss this statement, explaining the nature and effective section 31 of the Companies Act 2014. Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the "Bank") to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the "Company") on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from fair-trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for 2,500,000 in 2017. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1,500,000 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the Managing Director and sole shareholder in the company, asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the bank and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount or into the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.description: Irish Diverse Holdings Limited ("Holco") is an Irish registered company… Company, Director.
questions.text: Irish Diverse Holdings Limited ("Holco") is an Irish registered company which has its headquarters in Cork. It does not itself trade but has a number of wholly-owned subsidiaries which do trade and which distribute the profit they make up to Holco. Bangladesh Cottons Limited is one of its subsidiaries (Subco) and it is also an Irish registered company. Subco operates in Bangladesh where it benefits from cheap local labour to dye clothing for multiple retailers worldwide. The directors of Subco are the same as the directors of Holco. Subco's board of directors meets in Ireland and directs local management at the factory in Bangladesh through the CEO who is native to Bangladesh and who lives close to the factory and manages it on a day-to-day basis. Last year there was a chemical leakage at Subco's factory in Bangladesh and as a result of this, a local river was severely polluted. Shelia, who is a lawyer representing a number of local families whose fishing livelihood was ruined on account of the leak, approaches you for advice as to whether the families have a cause of action against Holco. The reason why she is interested in suing Holco as well as Subco is because Subco recently distributed its profits up to Holco and has little cash reserves and the property from which Subco operates its factory in Bangladesh is in fact owned by Holco. You are required to advise Shelia on the law relating to disregarding separate legal personality in Ireland and on the chances of her clients' success in Irish company law if they are to sue Holco in the Irish courts. You must support your answer with relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The members of a company can give their sanction to a proposed course of action in four distinct ways: (a) by a…
questions.text: "The members of a company can give their sanction to a proposed course of action in four distinct ways: (a) by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held (whether physically or virtually); (b) by a unanimous written resolution or in the case of a single member company, by the written decision of the sole member; (c) by a majority written resolution; and (d) by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement." You are required to explain the operation of each of these four means and you must support your answer with references to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.description: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as…
questions.text: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance." You are required to discuss this statement, and to cite relevant case law and statutory provisions in support of your answer.

questions.description: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Limited (the "Company") which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company's business had been going downhill, so Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: (a) he removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company's premises and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare, although it does not appear that Freda paid for the stock; (b) he caused the Company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to her under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to the two dispositions identified at (a) and (b). Advise the liquidator. In support of your answer, you are required to cite relevant case law and statutory provisions. There are 10 marks available for answering (a) and 10 marks available for answering (b).

questions.description: "The key to a successful defence to an application to have a director restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act…
questions.text: "The key to a successful defence to an application to have a director restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 is to establish that the director acted honestly, responsibly and cooperated with the liquidator". You are required to discuss this statement, and you must cite relevant case law and statutory provisions in support of your answer.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the "Company") which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company, which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by Simon, although Paul has worked in the Company for 10 years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. There are three other directors, in addition to Paul, in the Company (all family members, making four directors in total). Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been "double-jobbing" for the last two years, selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Specifically, Simon has heard that Jackie has been buying second-hand trade-in cars from the Company through Paul at a discounted price before selling on those cars through the website at a considerable profit to herself and Paul. Simon has also learned that Paul has been using the Company's garage-workshop to service those cars late at night and on Sundays when the garage-workshop is closed, using spare parts belonging to the Company, but without reimbursing the Company for those parts. You are required to advise Simon on the following: (a) whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Paul, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; and (b) the remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breach of duties. In answering you must cite relevant case law and statutory provisions and apply them to the facts provided above.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"). The Company was established by their late mother Madge and each of them acquired their shares under Madge's will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because neither had cash on hand to buy her shares. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she has sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy and asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's constitution, you see section 95(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014 applies and that the directors of the company may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register the transfer of any shares. You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. You are required to cite relevant case and statutory provisions to support your answer.

questions.description: "The protection afforded to minority shareholders in Irish law is derived from both statute and the common law" You are…
questions.text: "The protection afforded to minority shareholders in Irish law is derived from both statute and the common law" You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite relevant case and statutory provisions in support of your answer.
